real_meaning: This pattern actually has TWO completely different meanings depending on formation, and that ambiguity is the main trap. The first usage (appearance/conjecture) means 'it looks like ~' or 'it seems ~' — you are reporting what your senses are telling you RIGHT NOW about something you can directly observe. The second usage (hearsay) means 'I heard that ~' or 'apparently ~' — you are passing along information you received from an outside source, distancing yourself from responsibility for its truth.

formation.rule: APPEARANCE (looks like): attach そうだ to the verb STEM (drop ます): <ruby>食<rt>た</rt></ruby>べ＋そうだ → <ruby>食<rt>た</rt></ruby>べそうだ; to い-adjective stem (drop い): <ruby>美<rt>おい</rt></ruby>し＋そうだ → <ruby>美<rt>おい</rt></ruby>しそうだ; to な-adjective stem directly: <ruby>元<rt>げん</rt></ruby><ruby>気<rt>き</rt></ruby>＋そうだ → <ruby>元<rt>げん</rt></ruby><ruby>気<rt>き</rt></ruby>そうだ. HEARSAY (I heard that): attach そうだ to the PLAIN FORM (dictionary/ta/nai form) of verbs or adjectives: <ruby>食<rt>た</rt></ruby>べる＋そうだ → <ruby>食<rt>た</rt></ruby>べるそうだ; <ruby>美<rt>おい</rt></ruby>しい＋そうだ → <ruby>美<rt>おい</rt></ruby>しいそうだ.
formation.common_mistake: Learners often use the plain form for appearance-conjecture, which accidentally flips the meaning to hearsay. Wrong (appearance): <ruby>雨<rt>あめ</rt></ruby>が<ruby>降<rt>ふ</rt></ruby>るそうだ (this means 'I heard it will rain', NOT 'it looks like rain'). Correct (appearance): <ruby>雨<rt>あめ</rt></ruby>が<ruby>降<rt>ふ</rt></ruby>りそうだ.

sentences.jp: この<ruby>ケーキ<rt>けーき</rt></ruby>、すごく<ruby>美<rt>おい</rt></ruby>しそう！
sentences.notes: Classic appearance usage — the speaker is reacting to what they can see and smell right in front of them; the dropped だ at the end is natural in casual speech.

sentences.jp: <ruby>田<rt>た</rt></ruby><ruby>中<rt>なか</rt></ruby>さんは<ruby>来<rt>らい</rt></ruby><ruby>週<rt>しゅう</rt></ruby><ruby>会<rt>かい</rt></ruby><ruby>社<rt>しゃ</rt></ruby>を<ruby>辞<rt>や</rt></ruby>めるそうです。
sentences.translation: I heard that Tanaka-san is quitting the company next week.
sentences.notes: Hearsay usage — the plain form <ruby>辞<rt>や</rt></ruby>める before そうです is the giveaway; the speaker learned this from someone else and is not claiming direct knowledge.

sentences.jp: <ruby>新<rt>しん</rt></ruby><ruby>型<rt>がた</rt></ruby>のシステムは<ruby>導<rt>どう</rt></ruby><ruby>入<rt>にゅう</rt></ruby>に<ruby>時<rt>じ</rt></ruby><ruby>間<rt>かん</rt></ruby>がかかるそうでございます。
sentences.translation: I understand that implementing the new system will take some time.
sentences.notes: Hearsay そうだ in a very formal register using でございます; this is how you politely relay secondhand information in a business or official setting.

sentences.jp: <ruby>彼<rt>かれ</rt></ruby>は<ruby>元<rt>げん</rt></ruby><ruby>気<rt>き</rt></ruby>そうだったのに、<ruby>実<rt>じつ</rt></ruby>は<ruby>相<rt>とう</rt></ruby><ruby>当<rt>とう</rt></ruby><ruby>辛<rt>つら</rt></ruby>かったそうだ。
sentences.translation: He looked fine, but apparently he was really struggling the whole time.
sentences.notes: Both usages appear in one sentence: <ruby>元<rt>げん</rt></ruby><ruby>気<rt>き</rt></ruby>そうだった (appearance conjecture, past) contrasts with <ruby>辛<rt>つら</rt></ruby>かったそうだ (hearsay) — a powerful example of how the two meanings can play off each other.

confused_with.contrast: ～らしい also means 'apparently' or 'seems like', but it signals that the speaker is making an inference based on indirect evidence or general impression — it's more like a reasoned guess than pure hearsay or direct sensory observation. ～ようだ means 'it appears that' based on evidence you have access to, often more objective-feeling than そうだ. Compare: <ruby>雨<rt>あめ</rt></ruby>が<ruby>降<rt>ふ</rt></ruby>りそうだ (the sky right now LOOKS like it's about to rain — immediate visual impression) vs. <ruby>雨<rt>あめ</rt></ruby>が<ruby>降<rt>ふ</rt></ruby>るらしい (it seems like it'll rain — based on a forecast, what someone said, or general signs). Use そうだ (appearance) when your senses are firing right now; use らしい when you're reasoning from clues or social information.

bunpro_tip: Nail the formation split first: STEM+そうだ = 'looks like' (your eyes are the source), PLAIN FORM+そうだ = 'I heard' (someone else is the source). A quick mnemonic: if the verb is already 'complete' (plain form), the information is already 'complete' — it came from outside. If the verb is cut short (stem), your observation is still in progress — you're watching it happen.
